title: Phosphorylation

infobox.value: is described as the "transfer of a phosphate group" from a donor to an acceptor[[1]](#cite_note-ana&physio-1) or the addition of a phosphate group to a molecule. A common phosphorylating agent (phosphate donor) is [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") and a common family of acceptor are [alcohols](/wiki/Alcohol_(Chemistry) "Alcohol (Chemistry)"):
is a process that breaks down glucose into 2 pyruvate molecules, using ATP and NADH as well as producing it.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Chemical process of introducing a phosphate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Phosporylation_of_a_serine_residue%2C_before_and_after_shot.png/250px-Phosporylation_of_a_serine_residue%2C_before_and_after_shot.png)](/wiki/File:Phosporylation_of_a_serine_residue,_before_and_after_shot.png)

[Serine](/wiki/Serine "Serine") in an amino acid chain, before and after phosphorylation.

In [biochemistry](/wiki/Biochemistry "Biochemistry"), **phosphorylation** is described as the "transfer of a phosphate group" from a donor to an acceptor[[1]](#cite_note-ana&physio-1) or the addition of a phosphate group to a molecule. A common phosphorylating agent (phosphate donor) is [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") and a common family of acceptor are [alcohols](/wiki/Alcohol_(Chemistry) "Alcohol (Chemistry)"):

:   [Adenosyl−O−PO2−O−PO2−O−PO3]4− + ROH → Adenosyl−O−PO2−O−PO3H]2− + [RO−P−O3]2−

This equation can be written in several ways that are nearly equivalent that describe the behaviors of various protonated states of ATP, ADP, and the phosphorylated product.
As is clear from the equation, a phosphate group per se is not transferred, but a phosphoryl group (PO3-). **Phosphoryl** is an [electrophile](/wiki/Electrophile "Electrophile").[[2]](#cite_note-Ahn-2)
This process and its inverse, [dephosphorylation](/wiki/Dephosphorylation "Dephosphorylation"), are common in [biology](/wiki/Biology "Biology").[[3]](#cite_note-Chen-2022-3) [Protein phosphorylation](/wiki/Protein_phosphorylation "Protein phosphorylation") often activates (or deactivates) many [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## ATP is produced by phosphorylation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phosphorylation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: ATP is produced by phosphorylation")]

Although most often discussed in terms of the *consumption* of ATP (GTP and others), phosphorylation must also be involved in the production of these energy-rich species. ATP is produced by:

* [oxidative phosphorylation](/wiki/Oxidative_phosphorylation "Oxidative phosphorylation") during aerobic respiration in the [mitochondrion](/wiki/Mitochondrion "Mitochondrion") from [adenosine diphosphate](/wiki/Adenosine_diphosphate "Adenosine diphosphate") (ADP).
* [substrate-level phosphorylation](/wiki/Substrate-level_phosphorylation "Substrate-level phosphorylation") during [glycolysis](/wiki/Glycolysis "Glycolysis") and the [Krebs cycle](/wiki/Krebs_cycle "Krebs cycle").
* By [photophosphorylation](/wiki/Photophosphorylation "Photophosphorylation") in the [chloroplasts](/wiki/Chloroplast "Chloroplast") of plant cells.

## Phosphorylation of glucose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phosphorylation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Phosphorylation of glucose")]

### Glucose metabolism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phosphorylation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Glucose metabolism")]

Phosphorylation of [sugars](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar") is often the first stage in their [catabolism](/wiki/Catabolism "Catabolism"). Phosphorylation allows cells to accumulate sugars because the phosphate group prevents the molecules from diffusing back across their [transporter](/wiki/Transport_protein "Transport protein"). Phosphorylation of [glucose](/wiki/Glucose "Glucose") is a key reaction in sugar metabolism. The chemical equation for the conversion of D-glucose to D-glucose-6-phosphate in the first step of [glycolysis](/wiki/Glycolysis "Glycolysis") is given by:

:   [D-glucose](/wiki/D-glucose "D-glucose") + ATP → D-[glucose 6-phosphate](/wiki/Glucose_6-phosphate "Glucose 6-phosphate") + ADP

:   [ΔG](/wiki/Gibbs_free_energy "Gibbs free energy")° = −16.7 kJ/mol (° indicates measurement at standard condition)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Glycolysis_Simple_Diagram.jpg/250px-Glycolysis_Simple_Diagram.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glycolysis_Simple_Diagram.jpg)

**Glycolysis** is a process that breaks down glucose into 2 pyruvate molecules, using ATP and NADH as well as producing it.

Glucose is converted to glucose-6-phosphate catalyzed by the enzyme [hexokinase](/wiki/Hexokinase "Hexokinase"). Fructose-6-phosphate is converted to [fructose 1,6-bisphosphate](/wiki/Fructose_1,6-bisphosphate "Fructose 1,6-bisphosphate"). This reaction is catalyzed by [phosphofructokinase](/wiki/Phosphofructokinase "Phosphofructokinase").

[Glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate](/wiki/Glyceraldehyde_3-phosphate "Glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate") is again phosphorylated to give [1,3-bisphosphoglycerate](/wiki/1,3-bisphosphoglycerate "1,3-bisphosphoglycerate"). This reaction is catalyzed by [glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase](/wiki/Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate_dehydrogenase "Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase") (GAPDH).

### Glycogen synthesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phosphorylation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Glycogen synthesis")]

The phosphorylation of glucose to glucose 6-phosphate has role in regulating [glycogen synthase](/wiki/Glycogen_synthase "Glycogen synthase").

Glucose is phosphorylated to glucose 6-phosphate to allow its transport across the membrane by ATP-D-glucose 6-[phosphotransferase](/wiki/Phosphotransferase "Phosphotransferase") and non-specific [hexokinase](/wiki/Hexokinase "Hexokinase") (ATP-D-hexose 6-phosphotransferase).[[6]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-6)[[7]](#cite_note-fasebj.org-7) Liver cells are freely permeable to glucose, and the initial rate of phosphorylation of glucose is the rate-limiting step in glucose metabolism by the liver.[[6]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-6)

The liver's crucial role in controlling blood sugar concentrations by breaking down glucose into carbon dioxide and glycogen is characterized by the negative [Gibbs free energy](/wiki/Gibbs_free_energy "Gibbs free energy") (ΔG) value, which indicates that this is a point of regulation with.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] The hexokinase enzyme has a low [Michaelis constant](/wiki/Michaelis_constant "Michaelis constant") (Km), indicating a high affinity for glucose, so this initial phosphorylation can proceed even when glucose levels at nanoscopic scale within the blood.

The phosphorylation of glucose can be enhanced by the binding of [fructose 6-phosphate](/wiki/Fructose_6-phosphate "Fructose 6-phosphate") (F6P), and lessened by the binding [fructose 1-phosphate](/wiki/Fructose_1-phosphate "Fructose 1-phosphate") (F1P). Fructose consumed in the diet is converted to F1P in the liver. This negates the action of F6P on glucokinase,[[6]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-6) which ultimately favors the forward reaction. The capacity of liver cells to phosphorylate fructose exceeds capacity to metabolize fructose-1-phosphate. Consuming excess fructose ultimately results in an imbalance in liver metabolism, which indirectly exhausts the liver cell's supply of ATP.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[Allosteric activation](/wiki/Allosteric_activation "Allosteric activation") by glucose-6-phosphate, which acts as an effector, stimulates glycogen synthase, and glucose-6-phosphate may inhibit the phosphorylation of glycogen synthase by [cyclic AMP](/wiki/Cyclic_adenosine_monophosphate "Cyclic adenosine monophosphate")-stimulated [protein kinase](/wiki/Protein_kinase "Protein kinase").[[7]](#cite_note-fasebj.org-7)

### Other processes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phosphorylation&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Other processes")]

Phosphorylation of glucose is imperative in processes within the body. For example, phosphorylating glucose is necessary for insulin-dependent [mechanistic target of rapamycin](/wiki/Mechanistic_target_of_rapamycin "Mechanistic target of rapamycin") pathway activity within the heart. This further suggests a link between intermediary metabolism and cardiac growth.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Protein phosphorylation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phosphorylation&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Protein phosphorylation")]

Main article: [Protein phosphorylation](/wiki/Protein_phosphorylation "Protein phosphorylation")

[Protein phosphorylation](/wiki/Protein_phosphorylation "Protein phosphorylation") is the most common [post-translational modification](/wiki/Posttranslational_modification "Posttranslational modification") in eukaryotes. The most common phospho-amino acid residues are those serine, threonine, and tyrosine at a ratio of 1800:200:1.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) Phosphorylation of the side chains of these residues through [phosphoester bond](/wiki/Phosphodiester_bond "Phosphodiester bond") formation, on [histidine](/wiki/Histidine "Histidine"), [lysine](/wiki/Lysine "Lysine") and [arginine](/wiki/Arginine "Arginine") through [phosphoramidate bonds](/wiki/Phosphoramidate "Phosphoramidate"), and on [aspartic acid](/wiki/Aspartic_acid "Aspartic acid") and [glutamic acid](/wiki/Glutamic_acid "Glutamic acid") through mixed [anhydride linkages](/wiki/Organic_acid_anhydride "Organic acid anhydride").

Protein phosphorylation is common on human non-canonical amino acids, including motifs containing phosphorylated histidine, aspartate, glutamate, [cysteine](/wiki/Cysteine "Cysteine"), arginine and lysine in HeLa cell extracts.[[11]](#cite_note-ReferenceC-11) Histidine phosphorylates at both the 1 and 3 N-atoms of the [imidazole](/wiki/Imidazole "Imidazole") ring.[[12]](#cite_note-ncbi.nlm.nih.gov-12)[[13]](#cite_note-https-13) Phospho-tyrosine is much more stable than phospho-serine and -threonine which are in turn more stable than other phospho-amino acids,[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) hence the analysis of phosphorylated histidine (and other non-canonical amino acids) using standard biochemical and mass spectrometric approaches is much more challenging[[11]](#cite_note-ReferenceC-11)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-15) and special procedures and separation techniques are required for their preservation alongside classical Ser, Thr and Tyr phosphorylation.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The prominent role of protein phosphorylation in [biochemistry](/wiki/Biochemistry "Biochemistry") is illustrated by the many publication on the subject (as of March 2015, the [MEDLINE](/wiki/MEDLINE "MEDLINE") database returns over 240,000 articles, mostly on *protein* phosphorylation).
